mnemonics: LLR (Left, Level, Right) - A binary tree node has a Left child pointer, stores its Level (or data), and a Right child pointer.
A Binary Tree is like a Family Tree: Each Parent can have a maximum of two Children (left and right).
keyPoints: A node in a linked representation contains three parts: a data element, a pointer to the left child, and a pointer to the right child.
An array representation uses a level-order traversal. For a node at index 'i', its left child is at index 2i+1 and its right child is at index 2i+2.
The root node is the starting point of the tree in linked representation and is at index 0 in array representation.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to set the left or right child pointers to NULL (or nullptr) for nodes that do not have those children, which can lead to errors when traversing.
In array representation, incorrectly calculating child indices (e.g., using 2i and 2i+1 for a 0-indexed array instead of 2i+1 and 2i+2), which is a common off-by-one error.
Assuming an array representation is always a complete binary tree and not accounting for wasted memory space when the tree is not complete or full.
